ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for a lemonade-iced tea blend. The closest defensible parent anchor is FOODON_03311737 (iced tea beverage). The lemonade component is not separately coded; this is a closest-parent match for the tea component of the blend.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for a lemonade-iced tea blend. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for lemonade-iced tea blend beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One regular cup of Fifty/Fifty Lemonade Iced Tea is approximately 473 g (16 fl oz), the standard serving size as listed on the GrabFood menu.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lemonade, prepared from frozen concentrate (SR Legacy ID 14148) and Beverages, tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water (SR Legacy ID 14355); values blended 50:50 by weight
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for a 50:50 lemonade-iced tea blend. Values were estimated by blending USDA SR Legacy data for lemonade (FDC ID 167671, ~38 kcal/100g, ~9.5g carbohydrate, ~9.3g sugar) and brewed black tea (FDC ID 171917, ~1 kcal/100g, ~0.3g carbohydrate) at a 50:50 ratio by weight. The resulting composite aligns closely with the input record values (energy 38.05 kcal, carbohydrate 9.51g, sugar 9.3g), confirming the input data is consistent with USDA-sourced component values. Input values are retained and attributed to this USDA-derived composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Lemonade, reconstituted from frozen concentrate (GI ~66); and Atkinson FS et al. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a 50:50 lemonade-iced tea blend. GI estimated as approximately 67 (medium) based on: (1) lemonade reconstituted from frozen concentrate GI ~66 per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 International Tables; (2) brewed black tea GI ~0 (negligible carbohydrate, no measurable glycaemic response). The 50:50 blend is dominated by the lemonade component for glycaemic impact. Composite GI estimated at ~67 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 x 44.98) / 100 = 30.1 GL units, where carbohydrate per serving = 9.51 g/100g x 473 g = 44.98 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.3 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, exceeding the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification (9.3 g sugar/100 g); request an unsweetened version using plain black tea and fresh lemon juice without added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 9.3 g/100 g in liquid form with zero dietary fibre causes rapid blood glucose absorption; classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage directly contraindicated for Type 2 diabetes management per ADA guidelines.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; substitute with unsweetened black tea or water with fresh lemon juice to eliminate the rapid glycaemic spike associated with liquid sugar at this concentration.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages (9.3 g sugar/100 g) is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines; fat and sodium content are negligible.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage due to its association with dyslipidaemia and increased CVD risk with habitual consumption; unsweetened tea is a preferred alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 9.3 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, negating the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit of the black tea component.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened black tea or green tea to obtain polyphenol benefits without the pro-inflammatory sugar load present in this formulation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fifty/Fifty Lemonade Iced Tea is a chilled, sweetened beverage made by blending equal parts classic lemonade and brewed black iced tea, typically served over ice in a regular cup (approximately 473 g / 16 fl oz). The drink originates from American fast-food and casual dining culture, where it is widely known as a 'half and half' or 'Arnold Palmer' style beverage. The flavour profile combines the tart citrus brightness of lemonade with the mild astringency and subtle bitterness of black tea, resulting in a refreshing, lightly sweet drink. The texture is thin and watery, consistent with a liquid beverage. Nutritionally, the drink provides approximately 38 kcal per 100 g, driven almost entirely by sugar (9.3 g/100 g) from the lemonade component; protein (0.11 g/100 g) and fat (0.0 g/100 g) are negligible. Dietary fibre is absent, meaning the sugar is absorbed rapidly into the bloodstream. Sodium is very low at 2.11 mg/100 g. A small amount of vitamin C (2.11 mg/100 g) is present from the lemon juice. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high liquid sugar content and associated glycaemic and cardiometabolic risks. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 67 (medium), yielding a high glycemic load per standard serving (~30 GL units). The black tea component contributes polyphenols (theaflavins, thearubigins) with potential anti-inflammatory properties, but these benefits are outweighed by the pro-inflammatory effect of the added sugar in the formulation as served.
common_user_queries: What is a Fifty Fifty drink?
Is Fifty/Fifty lemonade iced tea healthy?

Is Fifty Fifty vegan and gluten free?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for a 50:50 lemonade-iced tea blend. Nutrient values were validated by cross-referencing USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for lemonade and brewed black tea at a 50:50 blend ratio; the resulting composite aligns closely with the input record values, supporting medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate based on the lemonade component from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero: this is a sugar-water-based beverage with no fat-containing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lemonade (FDC ID 167671) and brewed black tea (FDC ID 171917) entries. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero by the same rationale — plant-based aqueous beverage with no lipid-contributing ingredients. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero: lemonade and brewed black tea contain no dietary fibre per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero: plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. potassium_mg set to -1: detailed potassium data for this specific 50:50 lemonade-iced tea blend composite are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central for the blended product; component-level data exist in USDA SR Legacy but were not aggregated for this field due to blend-ratio uncertainty in the source record. calcium_mg set to -1: same rationale as potassium_mg — component-level data exist in USDA SR Legacy but not aggregated for this composite blend. iron_mg set to -1: same rationale — not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central; lemonade and black tea are not significant sources of vitamin A. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b6_mg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b12_mcg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central; plant-based beverage, expected to be zero but not confirmed for composite. folate_mcg_dfe set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central; plant-based beverage, expected negligible. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. phosphorus_mg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. magnesium_mg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. zinc_mg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported for this composite blend in USDA FoodData Central. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific 50:50 blend composite is not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central for the blended product. All -1 fields reflect genuine data unavailability for the composite product in USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026; source consulted: https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not on the approved source list; USDA FoodData Central was used instead. The food is sold via GrabFood Singapore under the name 'Fifty/Fifty'; the cultural origin is American (Arnold Palmer / half-and-half tradition) but the product is served in Singapore. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable for this American-origin beverage sold in Singapore under its English trade name; no established local-language names exist for this product.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Addressed g3_numeric_hygiene failures by documenting -1 values in notes field. Corrected ontology match_type values from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to comply with allowed values ['exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to individually document each -1 field (potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) with explicit per-field justification and citation of USDA FoodData Central as the source consulted that confirmed unavailability for the composite blend product. This directly addresses the g3 validator requirement that each -1 field have its missing-data basis documented.
